english: three great jewels, or as part of the godhead (in which latter sense the word now is usually understood) cf. dkon-mčóg (dkon mchog); dge-ʽdun-dpal-čéṅ (dge 'dun dpal chen) Mahāsāṅghika, n. of a Hinayāna school Tar., Was.; dge-ʽdun-grúb-pa (dge 'dun grub pa) n. p., the first Dalai Lama about the year 1400; dge-ʽdun-rgyá-mtśo (dge 'dun rgya mtsho) n. of the second Dalai Lama, v. Köpp. II., 131. — dge-ldán (dge ldan) virtuous; dge-ldan-pa (dge ldan pa) n. of the most numerous sect of Lamas, founded by Tsoṅkʽapa; it is also called dge-lúgs-pa (dge lugs pa), or dga-ldán-pa (dga' ldan pa) from Galdan, a monastery near Lhaśa which, as well as Sera and Ḍa-puṅ, belongs to his sect. The Lamas of this community wear for the most part yellow garments; they are said to approach nearer to perfection in mysticism (the highest aim of Buddhist priests) than any other sect, since they apply themselves more systematically to the preparatory studies of morality etc. — dge-sdig (dge sdig) for dge-ba daṅ sdig-pa (dge ba dang sdig pa). — dge-sbyóṅ (dge sbyong) Ssk. श्रमण a Buddhist ascetic, or mendicant friar, Burn. I. 275. Köpp. I., 330. — dge-sbyóṅ (dge sbyong) seems to have corresponded in its original acceptation to our conception of piety, sanctification and practical religion, but in later times the sense of expertness in the art of meditation was attached also to this word, as: dge-sbyóṅ pʽel (dge sbyong phel) (this man's) expertness increases, is making progress Mil. — dge-rtsa (dge rtsa) instead of dge-baí rtsá-ba (dge ba'i rtsa ba) v. above. — dge-rtsis (dge rtsis) the amount of virtue, the sum of merit, dge-rtsis rgyás-pa (dge rtsis rgyas pa) a considerable amount of merit. — dge-tśúl (dge tshul) 1. a young monk; in the older writings it may be understood as novice; 2. in later literature it denotes the degree next to the dge-bsnyén (dge bsnyen), being that of a subordinate or under-priest, Köpp. II. 252, 335. Schl. 162.; dge-tsul-ma (dge tsul ma) a young nun, a novice. — dge-mtśán (dge mtshan) a lucky omen Glr. — dge-gźón (dge gzhon) v. dge-rgán (dge rgan). — dge-gyóg (dge gyog) (seems to be pronounced *ger-yóg* (*ger yog*) in col. language) constable, beadle, a servant of the tśógs-čéṅ zal-ṅó (tshogs chen zhal ngo), or chief-justice of Sera and other monasteries. — dge-las (dge las) a good deed or action, but by later writers also applied to magic ceremonies and the like. — dge-lúgs-pa (dge lugs pa) v. dge-ldán-pa (dge ldan pa). — dge-légs (dge legs) good fortune, prosperity Glr. — dge-slóṅ (dge slong) Gelong, 1. originally 'beggar of virtue', mendicant friar, भिक्षु one that has entirely renounced the world and become a Buddhist priest, 2. in later writings the highest clerical degree, a priest that has received the highest ordination, v. Köpp. I., 335. The Gelong is bound to observe all the 233 commandments of the so-sór tʽar-pai mdo (so sor thar pa'i mdo). — dge-slób-ma (dge slob ma) a young nun Cs. — dge-bśés (dge bshes) 1. v. bśes-gnyén (bshes gnyen). 2. n. of priests or monks. — dge-sloṅ-śiṅ (dge slong shing) is said to be a provincial name of the cedar, Cedrus Deodara.
also dgán-la (dgan la), on, upon, in, at Ts.
= gyo-ba (gyo ba), to prepare, (food), kʽúr-ba dgér-ba (khur ba dger ba) to bake pastry; *tú'-ma gér-wa* (*tu' ma ger wa*) C. = ʽtśód-pa ('tshod pa).
in Lexx. explained by dúm-bur (dum bur), to divide (?).
a species of antelope, living on high mountains, Procapra picticaudata Hodgson, v. Hook. II. 157 and 139; dgó-ba-mo (dgo ba mo) the female of this antelope Cs.
Lexx. w.e.; dgog-tíṅ (dgog ting) pestle C.

also dgóṅ(s)-mo (dgong(s) mo), Sch. dgóṅ(s)-ka (dgong(s) ka) 1. evening, dgoṅs-yčig (dgongs gcig) one evening, once on an evening Glr.; naṅ re dgoṅs re (nang re dgongs re) every morning and evening; *goṅs-zán* (*gongs zan*) W., *góṅs-zé* (*gongs ze*) C., resp. dgoṅs-gsól (dgongs gsol) evening-meal, supper; dgoṅs-ťúg (dgongs thug) resp. evening-soup; dgóṅs-su (dgongs su) Dzl., dgóṅs-mo (dgongs mo) and dgoṅs (dgongs) Glr. in the evening; dgoṅs daṅ ťo-ráṅs (dgongs dang tho rangs) in the evening and in the morning Med. frq.; dgoṅs ʽbáb-pa (dgongs 'bab pa) to hold an evening's rest, to take up night-quarters. — 2. supper C. — 3. a day's journey, dgoṅs-źág (dgongs zhag) col. id.; rta-dgóṅs (rta dgongs) a day's journey for one travelling on horseback, lug-dgóṅs (lug dgongs) a day's journey for a drove of sheep.
resp. for sém-pa (sem pa), snyám-pa (snyam pa) etc., and sems (sems), blo (blo) etc. I. vb.
